item.title: Being Fired in Germany: My Labor Court Experience and What You Need to Know
item.description:
Being Fired in Germany: My Labor Court Experience
#

Nobody tells you how this works. I lived through it — here’s every detail.

Getting fired in Germany works very differently from Turkey — or most other countries. There’s a strict legal process, formal notification requirements, and rights you will lose if you don’t act on them. This is my personal account of what happened, step by step.

item.title: Germany Job Seeker Visa: My Personal Experience and Everything You Need to Know
item.description:
Germany’s Job Seeker Visa: My Personal Experience and Everything You Need to Know
#

I researched it on YouTube, applied, received a response a year later. Here’s exactly what happened — and what you should do today.

One of the most common questions from people in Turkey (and across the world) who want to move to Germany is: “How can I go there to find work if I don’t already have a job?”

item.title: Netherlands vs Luxembourg vs Germany: Why I Chose Germany
item.description:
Netherlands vs Luxembourg vs Germany: Why I Chose Germany
#

I researched all three seriously. Here’s the side-by-side comparison — and the honest reasons behind my final decision.

Before I moved to Munich, I spent a long time looking at three countries: the Netherlands, Luxembourg, and Germany. I had job interviews in all three. I received sponsorship offers. I ran the numbers.

item.title: Working and Living in Luxembourg: Europe's Best Kept Secret?
item.description:
Working & Living in Luxembourg: Europe’s Best Kept Secret?
#

Free public transport. Low taxes. A country where half the population are immigrants. Here’s the full picture.

Luxembourg doesn’t come up often in conversations about where to move in Europe. It should.

I had a job interview there. I researched the country seriously. And while I ultimately chose Germany, what I found in Luxembourg surprised me — both the advantages and the very real challenges.

item.title: Working and Living in the Netherlands: What I Learned Before Moving
item.description:
Working & Living in the Netherlands: What I Learned Before Moving
#

I applied for jobs there, got a sponsorship offer, and researched everything. Here’s what nobody tells you.

The Netherlands was my first serious target when I decided to move abroad. I spent months researching, applied to companies, and got far enough in the process to receive a sponsorship offer. I didn’t end up moving there — but I learned an enormous amount along the way.

item.title: Quality of Life and Happiness: The Final Verdict
item.description:
Quality of Life & Happiness: The Final Verdict
#

After everything — is Germany better? The honest answer is more complicated than yes or no.

This is the closing article in the Turkey vs Germany series. If you’ve read the others, you’ll know I’ve tried to be honest about both sides throughout. This is where I pull it all together.

item.title: Climate and Seasons: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Climate & Seasons: Turkey vs Germany
#

I knew German winters would be grey. I didn’t know how grey. Or how beautiful the summers would be.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Sun as Default
#

Turkey’s climate varies dramatically by region — the Aegean and Mediterranean coasts offer warm, dry summers and mild winters; the Black Sea region is lush and rainy; central Anatolia gets genuine cold snaps in winter.

item.title: Growing Up Between Two Cultures: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Growing Up Between Two Cultures: Turkey vs Germany
#

My child speaks three languages and belongs fully to neither country. I’m not sure that’s a problem.

The Gift Nobody Talks About Enough
#

Children who grow up between two cultures get something most people never have: the ability to see any society from the outside. They know, instinctively, that the way things are done where they live is not the only way things can be done.

item.title: Primary School: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Primary School: Turkey vs Germany
#

No homework until age 10. I had to read that twice.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Academic Pressure, Early
#

Turkish primary education begins at age 6 and places significant emphasis on academic performance from early on. Homework, exams, and ranking become part of a child’s life quickly. The pressure to perform well — to secure a place in a good secondary school, then university — begins earlier than many parents would choose.

item.title: Childcare and the Kita System: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Childcare & the Kita System: Turkey vs Germany
#

Finding a Kita spot in Munich is harder than finding a flat. But the system, when you’re in it, is remarkable.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Family First, State Second
#

In Turkey, childcare is predominantly family-based. Grandparents — especially grandmothers — play a central role in raising young children. This is cultural expectation as much as practical arrangement.

item.title: Work Life and Work-Life Balance: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Work Life & Work-Life Balance: Turkey vs Germany
#

In Turkey, you work hard and stay late. In Germany, you leave on time — and nobody apologises for it.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Hustle Culture with Heart
#

Turkish work culture is intense. Long hours are common and often expected. Being seen to work hard — staying late, being available, putting in visible effort — carries cultural weight. The boundary between work and personal life is porous.

item.title: Cars and Traffic Culture: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Cars & Traffic Culture: Turkey vs Germany
#

The Autobahn has no speed limit. Turkish drivers have no limits either. But the difference is everything.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Expressive, Fast, and Unpredictable
#

Driving in Turkey is an experience. Traffic rules exist but are interpreted creatively. Lane discipline is loose. Horns are communication tools. Pedestrian crossings are suggestions.

item.title: Elderly Care and Family Structure: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Elderly Care & Family Structure: Turkey vs Germany
#

In Turkey, family takes care of family. In Germany, the state steps in. Neither model is perfect.

🇹🇷 Turkey: The Family as Safety Net
#

In Turkish culture, caring for elderly parents is not a policy decision — it’s a moral expectation. When parents age, children take responsibility. Grandparents often move in with their children’s families, or live very close by. The extended family is the primary support structure.

item.title: Being Turkish and Muslim in Germany: What It Is Actually Like
item.description:
Being Turkish & Muslim in Germany: What It’s Actually Like
#

The headlines don’t tell the full story. Here’s my day-to-day experience.

🇹🇷 The Baseline
#

In Turkey, your cultural and religious identity is the default. You don’t have to explain Ramadan, justify your food choices, or navigate whether your name will affect how you’re perceived. You exist in a society that was built around the same culture and faith you grew up in.

item.title: The Language Barrier: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
The Language Barrier: Turkey vs Germany
#

You can survive in Munich with English. But surviving and living are different things.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Your Language, Your World
#

Living in Turkey as a Turkish speaker means the world is fully open to you. Every bureaucratic process, every social interaction, every joke, every piece of paperwork — you navigate it all with complete fluency.

item.title: Social Life and Making Friends: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Social Life & Making Friends: Turkey vs Germany
#

“Germans are cold.” Is it true? It’s more complicated than that.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Warm, Fast, and Deep
#

Turkish social culture is famously warm. Strangers become friends quickly. Hospitality is almost a competitive sport — guests must be fed, tea must be offered, no one leaves without feeling welcomed.

item.title: Housing and Renting: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Housing & Renting: Turkey vs Germany
#

Finding a flat in Munich is one of the hardest things I’ve done. Here’s the full picture.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Flexible but Volatile
#

Renting in Turkey is relatively straightforward in process — you find a place, negotiate with the landlord, sign a contract, move in. Personal relationships matter: landlords often prefer tenants they know or who come recommended.

item.title: Food and Eating Out: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Food & Eating Out: Turkey vs Germany
#

Turkish cuisine is world-famous for a reason. But German food culture has surprises of its own.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Food as Love Language
#

In Turkey, food is central to everything — family, hospitality, identity, celebration. Turkish cuisine is genuinely one of the richest in the world: fresh ingredients, regional variety, generations of technique passed down through families.

item.title: Shopping and Daily Errands: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Shopping & Daily Errands: Turkey vs Germany
#

Sunday everything is closed. Yes, really. Here’s how to adapt — and what you might actually prefer.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Open, Loud, and Always Available
#

Shopping in Turkey is a full sensory experience. Markets are open late, corner shops (bakkal) are open almost around the clock, and if you run out of something at 11pm, there’s almost always somewhere to go.

item.title: Health System: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Health System: Turkey vs Germany
#

From walking into any hospital to waiting weeks for a specialist — here’s what actually changed when I moved

Healthcare is one of those things you don’t think about until you need it. Moving countries means rebuilding your entire relationship with the medical system from scratch — new doctors, new insurance, new language, new rules.

item.title: Turkey vs Germany: An Honest Comparison After Making the Move
item.description:
Turkey vs Germany: An Honest Comparison After Making the Move
#

Not a travel guide. Not a rant. Just what I’ve actually experienced living in both countries.

I want to be clear upfront: this isn’t about which country is “better.” It’s about what I personally found different, harder, easier, worse, and surprisingly better — from both sides. Because Germany has real frustrations too.

item.title: Why I Left Turkey: The Decision That Changed Everything
item.description:
Why I Left Turkey: The Decision That Changed Everything
#

A personal account of weighing family, freedom, and the future — from someone who actually made the move

Moving to another country is never just a practical decision. It’s emotional, messy, and deeply personal. I’ve been asked many times: “Why did you leave?” This is my honest answer.

item.title: Trust as a Way of Life: What Germany Taught Me About Believing in People
item.description:
Trust as a Way of Life: What Germany Taught Me About Believing in People
#

Living in Munich as an expat from Turkey — small details that changed how I see society

Germany surprised me. Not with its punctuality, not with its efficiency — but with something quieter and harder to name: trust. The kind of trust that doesn’t ask for proof. The kind that assumes you’ll do the right thing, even when no one is watching.

item.title: Regensburg Travel Guide | UNESCO Medieval City 1.5 Hours from Munich (2025)
item.description: Regensburg is Germany’s best-preserved medieval city and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Steinerne Brücke, a massive cathedral, Italian-style tower houses and the Danube riverbank — a world apart. 1.5 hours from Munich.

item.title: Ingolstadt Travel Guide | Danube City 1 Hour from Munich (2025)
item.description: Ingolstadt is known as Audi’s headquarters but its historic center is far richer. A city on the Danube River with medieval walls, the Bavarian Army Museum and an unexpected Frankenstein connection. 1 hour from Munich.

item.title: Neuschwanstein Castle Guide | Disney's Inspiration | Practical Tips (2025)
item.description: Neuschwanstein is Bavaria’s and probably Germany’s most visited structure — 1.5 million visitors per year. When to go and how to avoid crowds, how to buy tickets — 2025 practical guide.

item.title: Füssen Travel Guide | Alpine Medieval Town Beyond Neuschwanstein (2025)
item.description: Everyone goes to Füssen for Neuschwanstein — but leaves without seeing the town itself. This guide is for the city: Hohes Schloss with its trompe l’œil windows, the Altstadt, the Lech River canyon and nearby Forggensee.

item.title: Lindau Travel Guide | Island City on Lake Constance | 2 Hours from Munich (2025)
item.description: Lindau is a small island city on Lake Constance (Bodensee) in Bavaria, neighboring Austria and Switzerland. Harbor lion, colorful houses, lake views. 2 hours from Munich with Deutschlandticket.

item.title: Augsburg Travel Guide | Roman City 35 Minutes from Munich (2025)
item.description: Augsburg is a historic city 35 minutes from Munich with over 2,000 years of Roman history, and home to the world’s oldest social housing project — the Fuggerei, still operating since 1516.

item.title: Salzburg Travel Guide | Mozart's City 1.5 Hours from Munich (2025)
item.description: Salzburg is a 1.5-hour train journey from Munich — Mozart’s birthplace and the city where Sound of Music was filmed. Deutschlandticket takes you to the border free — a few euros more and you’re in Austria.

item.title: Memmingen Travel Guide | Medieval City 1.5 Hours from Munich (2025)
item.description: Memmingen is one of Bavaria’s least-known but best-preserved medieval towns. Near-complete city walls, Marktplatz, half-timbered buildings — a small but complete open-air museum. 1.5 hours from Munich with Deutschlandticket.

item.title: Christmas and New Year in Germany | Munich Christmas Markets & Silvester (2025)
item.description: Christmas in Germany is very different from what you might expect. Weihnachten is quiet and family-focused; Silvester is surprisingly loud. From Munich’s Christmas markets to last year’s Silvester experience — German winter culture through expat eyes.

item.title: Deutschlandticket Day Trips from Munich | Bavaria with Kids (2025)
item.description: For 49 EUR per month you can take all local and regional trains across Germany. From Munich to Füssen, Garmisch, Salzburg, Starnberg Lake — routes we tested with two kids aged 1 and 5 and a double stroller.

item.title: Renting an Apartment in Germany | The Reality of Finding Housing in Munich (2025)
item.description: Finding a rental in Munich is one of Germany’s most difficult bureaucratic processes. Schufa score, employment contract, reference letters and hundreds of applications — it gets even harder for foreigners. First-hand experience.

item.title: Can You Drive in Germany with a Bulgarian License? | Turkish→Bulgarian Transfer Reality (2025)
item.description: I obtained Bulgarian citizenship and converted my Turkish license to a Bulgarian one. Sofia KAT centers, mandatory interpreter, 30-day wait and then the clear answer from Germany: no, it doesn’t work. The EU directive doesn’t allow it.

item.title: Driving License Exchange in Germany | The Reality of Turkish & Bulgarian Licenses
item.description: Failing to plan the driving license exchange process early after moving to Germany can be costly. My experience trying to exchange a license via Bulgaria — and why it failed.

item.title: How to Find Cheap Flights | Pegasus, Google Flights and Real Tactics
item.description: Finding cheap flights is not a matter of luck — it’s about knowing the system. How to catch great deals with Pegasus campaign hours, Google Flights alerts and flexible route planning.

item.title: How to Get a Schengen Visa (2025) | Kozmos, Appointments and Real Experiences
item.description: Getting a Schengen visa after the pandemic has gotten harder. Greece visa via Kozmos, appointment-finding tactics and application as a relative of an EU citizen — summary of 5 successful applications.

item.title: Georgia Travel Guide: Tbilisi & Batumi | 5-Day Real Experience (2024)
item.description: A missed flight from Istanbul to Georgia, a land border crossing, pushing a stroller through Tbilisi and a taxi argument in Batumi. An unembellished, real 5-day Georgia experience.

item.title: Hidden Gems in Tbilisi and Batumi | 15 Places You Haven't Seen
item.description: Every Georgia travel guide tells you the same places. This article has a different Georgia: lesser-known neighborhoods, hidden cafés, local experiences and corners not shown to standard tourists.

item.title: Greece Travel Guide | Athens, Islands and Practical Tips (2024)
item.description: Greece is the address of both history and sunshine. Acropolis in Athens, turquoise sea on the islands. Where to start in Greece, which requires a Schengen visa, and how to plan a budget?

item.title: Switzerland Basel Travel Guide | One City, Three Countries: Switzerland, France, Germany (2024)
item.description: In Basel you can have breakfast in France in the morning and lunch in Germany. This city, standing at the exact intersection point of Switzerland, France and Germany, means three countries in a single trip.

item.title: Switzerland Zurich Travel Guide | Things to Do, Transport & Budget (2024)
item.description: Zurich, Switzerland’s financial capital, is one of the world’s most expensive cities. But with the right planning, it’s possible to see the city’s best places without burning through your wallet.

item.title: Bosnia Herzegovina Sarajevo Travel Guide | Visa-Free, Budget & Historic (2024)
item.description: Sarajevo, with its Ottoman traces, war history and familiar faces, is a destination that is both close and deeply moving for travelers. Visa-free, affordable and just 1.5 hours by plane.

item.title: Kosovo Travel Guide | Pristina and Prizren | Visa-Free Balkan Route (2024)
item.description: Kosovo is one of the least explored countries in the Balkans. Visa-free, affordable and full of Ottoman traces. Pristina’s modern face and Prizren’s historic texture — two different experiences in one trip.

item.title: Skopje Travel Guide | North Macedonia Capital | Visa-Free (2024)
item.description: Skopje is one of the Balkans’ strangest and most interesting capitals. With giant statues, the Ottoman bazaar and the controversial Skopje 2014 architectural project, the city is like an open-air museum.

item.title: About Me
item.description:
Hello, Welcome to Share Hub!
#

This blog is dedicated to those living in Germany, planning to move to Europe, or looking to explore travel routes from the Balkans to the Caucasus.

You won’t find just official procedures here; you’ll find real-life experiences. Whether it’s the specific documents that tripped me up during my driver’s license exchange in Munich, how I secured a visa appointment, or navigating Tbilisi’s sulfur baths with two kids (aged 1 and 5) and a massive double stroller — I share it all exactly as it happened.

item.title: Contact
item.description:
For collaborations, questions, and feedback:

info (at) share-hub-eu.online

You can also share your experiences, suggestions, or corrections regarding the articles. I read and respond to every message.

📫 Email: info (at) share-hub-eu.online
